text: After this, David inquired of the LORD whether he should go up to one of the cities of Judah, and the LORD told him to go up to Hebron.
So David went up there with his two wives, Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail, the widow of Nabal of Carmel.
David also brought the men who were with him, each with his household, and they settled in the towns around Hebron.
The men of Judah came and anointed David king over the house of Judah; and he was told that the men of Jabesh-gilead had buried Saul.
David sent messengers to the men of Jabesh-gilead, blessing them for the kindness they showed Saul in burying him.
He asked the LORD to show them steadfast love and faithfulness, and said he too would reward them for what they had done.
He told them to be strong and valiant, for though Saul their lord was dead, the house of Judah had anointed him king over them.
Meanwhile Abner, the commander of Saul's army, took Ish-bosheth, Saul's son, and brought him over to Mahanaim.
Abner made him king over Gilead, the Ashurites, Jezreel, Ephraim, Benjamin, and all Israel.
Ish-bosheth, Saul's son, was forty years old when he began to reign over Israel, and he reigned two years; but the house of Judah followed David.
The time David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah was seven years and six months.
Abner and the servants of Ish-bosheth went out from Mahanaim to Gibeon.
Joab and the servants of David went out too, and they met at the pool of Gibeon, sitting down on opposite sides of it.
Abner said to Joab, 'Let the young men arise and compete before us,' and Joab agreed.
So twelve from Benjamin and Ish-bosheth's side, and twelve of David's servants, stood up and crossed over.
Each man seized his opponent's head and thrust his sword into his side, and they fell down together; so the place was called Helkath-hazzurim, at Gibeon.
A very fierce battle followed that day, and Abner and the men of Israel were beaten before the servants of David.
The three sons of Zeruiah were there — Joab, Abishai, and Asahel; and Asahel was as swift of foot as a wild gazelle.
Asahel pursued Abner, not turning to the right or the left in his chase.
Abner looked behind him and asked if it was Asahel, and he answered that it was.
Abner told him to turn aside and seize one of the young men and take his spoil, but Asahel would not stop pursuing him.
Abner warned him again to turn aside, asking why he should strike him down, since then he could not face Joab, Asahel's brother.
But Asahel refused to turn away, so Abner struck him in the stomach with the butt of his spear, and he died on the spot; everyone who came to that place stood still.
Joab and Abishai pursued Abner, and the sun was going down when they reached the hill of Ammah, before Giah, on the way to the wilderness of Gibeon.
The men of Benjamin gathered behind Abner and took their stand on top of a hill.
Abner called to Joab, asking if the sword must devour forever, and whether he did not know it would end bitterly, urging him to tell the people to stop pursuing their brothers.
Joab answered that had Abner not spoken, the people would have kept pursuing their brothers until morning.
So Joab blew the trumpet, and all the people stopped, pursuing Israel no more and fighting no more.
Abner and his men marched all that night through the Arabah, crossed the Jordan, marched through Bithron, and came to Mahanaim.
Joab returned from pursuing Abner and gathered all the people; nineteen of David's servants besides Asahel were missing.
But David's servants had struck down three hundred and sixty of Abner's men, the men of Benjamin.
They took up Asahel and buried him in his father's tomb at Bethlehem, and Joab and his men marched all night, arriving at Hebron by daybreak.
